claims.text: Allegedly, hidden inside the Hebrew Old Testament is a code that predicted Israeli PM Yitzhak Rabin's 1995 assassination — including the date and the killer's name — and Rabin had been warned a year in advance via a message citing this code.

evidence.quote:
rine prime
minister of Israel received an ominous
message

the code hidden within
the Bible not only predicted rabin's
assassination but also predicted the
date and even the name of the Killer

claims.rationale: Rabin's 1995 assassination is real. The 'warning letter' from Eliyahu Rips and Drosnin to Rabin in 1994 is documented; Drosnin claimed to have spotted Rabin's name and 'assassin will assassinate' on intersecting equidistant letter sequences. However, the Bible-code methodology has been thoroughly debunked (see Brendan McKay et al.) — equidistant letter sequence searches over any sufficiently long text yield superficially impressive matches by chance. Drosnin's specific Rabin prediction is real history; whether the Bible code is genuine prophecy is essentially false.

claims.text: Equidistant letter sequence (ELS / 'skip code') decoding has roots going back to 13th-century Rabbi Bachya ben Asher, who allegedly extracted the lunar month length (~29.53 days) from Genesis centuries before NASA confirmed it.

evidence.quote:
in the late
13th century Rabbi bakia B Asher found a
code hidden in the Book of Genesis

29.53 days now that's a significant
finding because the exact length of the
lunar month wasn't calculated until 1996
by NASA

claims.rationale: Bachya ben Asher (13th century, Spain) was a real Torah commentator. The specific claim that he derived 29.53 days from a Genesis ELS, and that the value was 'unknown until 1996 by NASA', is wildly false: the 29.53059-day synodic month was known with high precision since Babylonian astronomy (~5th century BCE) and was used to set the Hebrew calendar long before Bachya. The host's setup conflates rediscovery via skip-code with original discovery.

claims.text: The 1985 'Great Rabbis Experiment' by Eliyahu Rips, Doron Witztum, and Yoav Rosenberg used computer ELS searches on Genesis to find the names of 34 famous rabbis and their birth/death dates encoded in the text — and reportedly produced 166 such hits with 1-in-10-million odds against chance.
entities: organization:encyclopedia of great men in israel

evidence.quote:
in 1985 called the Great
rabbis experiment

in
total they found the names of 166 men
encoded in the Bible thousands of years
before any of them were born Dr rip said
the odds of this happening by chance was
about 1 in 10 million

claims.rationale: The 1994 Witztum-Rips-Rosenberg paper in Statistical Science is a real publication and reports the rabbi-name correlation. The 'experiment' actually dates to the late 1980s/early 1990s, with 1985 referring to Rips's earlier preliminary work. The 1-in-10-million odds figure comes from the paper's reported significance level for one specific test. However, the methodology was savaged in 1999 by McKay et al. who showed the result depended sensitively on choice of names and spelling — they reproduced equally significant 'codes' in War and Peace. Net: the experiment exists; the conclusion does not survive scrutiny.

claims.text: NSA senior cryptologic mathematician Harold Gans set out in the 1990s to disprove the Bible Code, but instead replicated and extended Rips's findings — going so far as to find the rabbis' birth and death cities — converting himself from skeptic to believer.

evidence.quote:
in 1997 Harold Gans a
senior cryptologic mathematician for the
NSA set out to disprove the Bible Code

Gans created his own computer
program to disprove the Bible Code

claims.rationale: Harold Gans was a real NSA cryptologist who became a Bible-code defender and lectured widely on it. The descriptive claim that he attempted to replicate and ended up endorsing is true. However, Gans's later 'cities' study has the same methodological flaws as the original — flexible name-matching, post-hoc selection of which spellings 'count' as hits. McKay's later analyses of Gans's data show similar selection-bias issues. So Gans really did believe; the underlying scientific validity remains contested-to-debunked.

dependencies.rationale: Independent replication used by host as additional support for the original finding

claims.text: In 1990, Dr. Rips claims to have used the Bible Code to predict that Saddam Hussein would attack Israel within 3 weeks — and Iraq's Russian-made Scud strike on Israel on January 18, 1990 (the third Shabbat of the Hebrew calendar) corroborated this prediction.

evidence.quote:
Russian missile and fire on
third Shabbat on January 18th 1990

claims.rationale: The host's date is wrong: Iraq's Scud attacks on Israel began on January 17/18, 1991 (during the Gulf War), not 1990. Saddam's invasion of Kuwait was in August 1990; the Scud attacks on Israel followed coalition intervention in January 1991. The Bible-code 'prediction' framed retroactively, after the events, with flexible spelling and skip-distance choice, has all the hallmarks of post-hoc pattern-finding. The factual scaffolding is sloppy and the predictive power claim is essentially unfounded.

claims.text: Journalist Michael Drosnin took Rips's research mainstream and built on it, eventually claiming the Bible Code predicted a nuclear war (he warned the Israeli and US governments in 2002 about a 2006 attack involving Bin Laden, Jerusalem, and New York) and that a stone obelisk buried in the Jordanian desert would unlock the entire code.

evidence.quote:
in 2002
Michael dnan believed that the Bible
held a hidden code that predicted an
impending nuclear war

Jen believed there was a
key to the entire Bible Code in the form
of a stone obisk hidden somewhere in the
Jordanian desert

claims.rationale: Drosnin really did write three Bible Code books (1997, 2002, 2010), really did warn governments about nuclear-war predictions involving 2006, and really did pursue an obelisk-excavation theory. The descriptive claim is accurate. The underlying claim — that the Bible encodes future events — is essentially false. The 2006 nuclear war did not occur; subsequent 'fulfillments' that didn't happen tend to be quietly dropped in popular accounts.

dependencies.rationale: Drosnin built on the same research that produced the Rabin warning

claims.text: Drosnin held a non-religious theory of the Bible Code's origin — believing it was authored not by God but by a sufficiently advanced intelligence (likely aliens using quantum-level computing) capable of seeing across time, who left the code to be deciphered exactly when computers existed to find it.

evidence.quote:
logic tells me that the Bible
was encoded to give us very important
information it was encoded certainly by
some intelligence that could see ahead
across
time it was encoded in such a way that
it would be found right now by a
technology that would exist for the
first time now the computer

claims.rationale: Drosnin did publicly state this exact view (intelligence capable of seeing across time, leaves code for the computer age). Descriptively accurate. The underlying claim — that the Old Testament was authored by a time-spanning alien intelligence — has no empirical support and is essentially conspiratorial cosmology dressed up as cryptography.

claims.text: The Jordanian government initially granted Drosnin permission to excavate the desert obelisk site but revoked the permit at the last minute due to shifting Middle Eastern political circumstances — and Drosnin died in June 2020 with the alleged 'proof' still unrecovered.

evidence.quote: the excavation permit was revoked

claims.rationale: Drosnin died on June 9, 2020, after years of pursuing the 'lisan' obelisk theory. The Jordan-permit story is documented in his last book and in interviews. Whether the permit was 'revoked' for political reasons or never seriously granted is unclear from the public record. The host's framing matches Drosnin's account but is single-sourced.

claims.text: Drosnin issued a famous challenge that 'if my critics can find the assassination of a prime minister in Moby Dick, I'll believe them' — and Australian mathematician Brendan McKay promptly did exactly that, finding Yitzhak Rabin's assassination encoded in Moby Dick multiple times (more often than in the Old Testament), along with Princess Diana's death and Robert Kennedy's assassination.

evidence.quote:
if my
critics can find a message about the
assassination of a prime minister in
Moby Dick

the assassination of ysac rabine in Moby
Dick

claims.rationale: This is one of the cleanest debunkings in modern statistics and is well-documented. McKay (1997) accepted Drosnin's challenge and produced multiple 'Moby Dick' ELS findings: Rabin's assassination, Indira Gandhi, Robert Kennedy, Trotsky, Princess Diana, etc. The clear methodological lesson: any sufficiently long text + flexible matching = arbitrary 'predictions'. McKay's work effectively ended Bible Code's claim to scientific status.

dependencies.rationale: Demonstrates the Rips/Drosnin methodology is statistically unsound

claims.text: Brendan McKay further weaponized the methodology against itself by 'finding' references to the September 11th attacks encoded in Vanilla Ice's 'Ice Ice Baby' lyrics — driving home the point that long texts plus flexible search yield arbitrary patterns.

evidence.quote:
McKay even used this technique to find
phrases about the September 11th attack
in song lyrics by Vanilla Ice

claims.rationale: McKay (and various imitators) really did do this kind of demonstration. It's now a standard pedagogical example in statistics courses — 'p-hacking' applied to ELS searches. The Vanilla Ice example is a popularized form of the broader point. Solid science communication via reductio ad absurdum.

dependencies.rationale: Same methodology of arbitrary-text reductio applied to a different (sillier) text

claims.text: A major technical objection: ancient Hebrew was written without vowels, so ELS matches use only consonants — giving researchers enormous flexibility in deciding what constitutes a 'match' and inflating apparent significance.

evidence.quote:
modern Hebrew uses
vowels the ancient written Hebrew
language did not so word matches were
found using only consonants

claims.rationale: Linguistically accurate: Biblical Hebrew was written in a consonantal script (with later vowel pointings — niqqud — added by the Masoretes from ~7th-10th centuries CE). Consonant-only ELS searches dramatically expand the search space and the number of plausible 'hits'. McKay and others have explicitly cited this as a key methodological flaw. Solid critique.

dependencies.rationale: Provides the technical mechanism behind why arbitrary 'codes' show up so easily

claims.text: A second technical problem: although the modern Old Testament text is nearly identical to the ancient version, comparison with the Dead Sea Scrolls reveals a small number of character differences — enough to wreck any computer-based skip-code that depends on exact letter positions.

evidence.quote:
another
problem is that although the text of the
Old Testament is almost identical to the
text from 2 or 3 thousand years ago it's
not a perfect match comparisons of the
current text to older copies like the
Dead Sea Scrolls show a discrepancy

claims.rationale: Textual scholars (Tov, Schiffman, etc.) have catalogued the differences between the Masoretic Text used in modern Bible Code research and the older Qumran versions: spelling variants, occasional word-order differences, and some textual variants on the order of a few letters per chapter in some books. Even small letter differences shift every subsequent ELS position downstream and would shatter the original code claims if they applied to a 'divinely-fixed' text.

dependencies.rationale: Even granting Rips's methodology, textual instability undermines the 'unchanged-since-antiquity' premise

claims.text: Dr. Rips, the original researcher, never accepted Drosnin's predictive use of the code — Rips believed Drosnin was seeing only what he wanted to see and manipulating the program to confirm pre-chosen outcomes — even while Rips himself maintained the original Great Rabbis result.

evidence.quote:
Dr Rip's work had
challengers in 1999 Australian
mathematician Brendan McCay published
his own paper in statistical science

claims.rationale: Rips publicly distanced himself from Drosnin's prophecy work in multiple statements during the 1990s, while continuing to defend the original Witztum-Rips-Rosenberg paper. This intra-camp split is well-documented and is a useful detail — the 'serious' Bible-code researchers viewed Drosnin's pop-prophecy as embarrassing, even while McKay et al. were dismantling all of it.

claims.text: AJ's verdict: he doesn't know whether the Bible contains real hidden messages or whether this is just human pareidolia, but mysteries deserve investigation regardless — invoking Proverbs 25:2 ('the glory of God to conceal a matter, the honor of kings to search it out') as the spirit of the inquiry.

evidence.quote:
I do know that there
are things in the universe beyond our
understanding

claims.rationale: Pure host editorial — invokes a Proverbs verse to authorize continued inquiry without committing to a specific truth-claim about Bible Codes. directTruth omitted because it's an opinion/values claim, not an empirical one.
